core_behavior.note: All three models agree on the core pagination behavior: fixed 20-item pages, 1-based page numbering, and returning total counts.

inputs.note: Model B explicitly lists non-integer, null, zero, and negative page values as invalid inputs and states no caller-configurable page size; Models A and C do not enumerate these invalid input types in the inputs field.

outputs.note: Model B includes additional output fields (page echo, page_size, has_previous_page, has_next_page) not present in Models A or C.

assumptions.note: Model A assumes the collection is non-empty, while Model B explicitly assumes empty collections are valid and return a normal response, and Model C assumes the collection is already populated.

failure_modes.note: Model A returns an error for page_number > total_pages; Model C returns an empty list with metadata for that case; Model B does not address page > total_pages as a failure mode; none of the models specify behavior for non-integer page numbers in the failure_modes field.

edge_cases.note: Model B's edge_cases field contains only two entries about empty collections, while Models A and C cover additional cases such as exactly 20 items, fewer than 20 items, and last-page partial results.

security_constraints.note: Model B has an empty security_constraints array while Models A and C specify access control, injection prevention, and sensitive data handling.

performance_constraints.note: Model B has an empty performance_constraints array while Models A and C specify response time targets and optimization strategies.

summary: OMI was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in 3 of the 8 fields, driven by Model B's empty security and performance sections and its sparse edge_cases coverage.
